_meta.description: Adversarial eval set for the brief council gate. Each case is evidence + a brief + the verdict the grader MUST return. FAIL cases are briefs containing the exact failure modes most likely to make Verity wrong or biased. PASS cases guard against an over-strict grader. The first 3 FAIL cases are real fabrications caught in the live 2026-06-10 brief.
_meta.rule: Every time the gate catches a new fabrication in production, add it here. The set only grows.
_meta.pass_criteria: scripts/run_brief_evals.py — every case must match its expected verdict, on BOTH grader models.

cases.evidence:
News stories given to the writer:
- "NDIS reform debate intensifies" [politics] — 8 sources

Vote records given to the writer:
- National Disability Insurance Scheme Amendment Bill 2026 - Consideration in Detail: defeated (11–85)
- National Disability Insurance Scheme Amendment Bill 2026 - Second Reading amendment: defeated (12–86)

what_happened: Parliament rejected amendments to the NDIS bill this week.
brief.what_it_means: NDIS reform is contested and changes will be slow.
brief.one_thing_to_know: That NDIS bill got fewer votes than Palmer United ever managed — a historic low for a major party amendment.

cases.why_fail: Palmer United comparison appears nowhere in evidence — invented specific.

cases.evidence:
News stories given to the writer:
- "Tax reform package announced" [economy] — 12 sources

Vote records given to the writer:
- Treasury Laws Amendment (Tax Reform No. 1) Bill 2026 and another - Reference to Committee - Adjourn debate: passed (91–48)

what_happened: Parliament passed the government's tax reform bill 91–48.
brief.what_it_means: New tax rules are now on the way for Australian households.
brief.one_thing_to_know: The bill passed with a comfortable margin, showing strong cross-party support.

cases.why_fail: The vote was a procedural adjournment/committee referral, not passage of the bill. THE canonical trap: procedural motions that look substantive.

cases.evidence:
News stories given to the writer:
- "NDIS costs under scrutiny" [politics] — 6 sources

Vote records given to the writer:
- NDIS Amendment Bill - Consideration in Detail: defeated (11–85)
- NDIS Amendment Bill - amendment 2: defeated (12–86)
- NDIS Amendment Bill - amendment 3: defeated (52–90)

what_happened: NDIS amendments were rejected across the board.
brief.what_it_means: The scheme's settings stay as they are for now.
brief.one_thing_to_know: The amendments couldn't even get 15 votes in a 98-seat parliament.

cases.why_fail: Fabricated chamber size (Australia's House has 151 seats, and the evidence shows 142 voting on one division) and misrepresents tallies (one amendment got 52).

cases.origin: SYNTHETIC — party-line vote with a defector
cases.evidence:
News stories given to the writer:
- "Live export phase-out bill divides regional MPs" [agriculture] — 7 sources

Vote records given to the writer:
- Export Control Amendment (Ending Live Sheep Exports) Bill - Second Reading: passed (78–65). Note: one Labor MP crossed the floor to vote no.

what_happened: The live sheep export phase-out cleared its second reading 78–65 with every Labor MP voting for it.
brief.what_it_means: The phase-out is a step closer to becoming law.
brief.one_thing_to_know: The 78–65 margin shows how contested agricultural policy has become.

cases.why_fail: Claims 'every Labor MP voting for it' when the evidence explicitly notes one Labor MP crossed the floor. Defectors are exactly what Verity exists to surface — erasing them is a core failure.

cases.origin: SYNTHETIC — gag motion described as a policy verdict
cases.evidence:
News stories given to the writer:
- "Climate target debate flares in Question Time" [climate] — 9 sources

Vote records given to the writer:
- Motions - Climate Change - That the member be no longer heard: passed (88–55)

what_happened: Parliament voted 88–55 against stronger climate targets.
brief.what_it_means: Climate ambition has stalled in this parliament.
brief.one_thing_to_know: The 88–55 result shows the climate majority has evaporated.

cases.why_fail: 'That the member be no longer heard' is a gag motion about debate procedure — it says NOTHING about climate policy positions. Reading policy meaning into procedural silencing votes is a known trap.

cases.evidence:
News stories given to the writer:
- "Housing affordability package introduced" [housing] — 10 sources

Vote records given to the writer:
- Housing Australia Future Fund Amendment Bill - Second Reading: passed (89–54)

what_happened: Labor's housing bill passed its second reading 89–54 while the Coalition voted to keep young Australians locked out of home ownership.
brief.what_it_means: The government is delivering on housing while the opposition obstructs.
brief.one_thing_to_know: Opposing housing supply has become the Coalition's brand this term.

cases.why_fail: Tallies are accurate but the framing assigns motive ('voted to keep young Australians locked out') and editorialises ('obstructs', 'become the Coalition's brand'). Neutrality product — this is partisan output wearing factual clothes.

cases.evidence:
News stories given to the writer:
- "Integrity commission powers reviewed" [justice] — 5 sources

Vote records given to the writer:
- National Anti-Corruption Commission Amendment Bill - Second Reading: passed (72–41). Note: 38 members did not vote.

what_happened: The anti-corruption bill passed 72–41, with 38 MPs refusing to support it by staying away.
brief.what_it_means: Integrity reform has more enemies than the vote count shows.
brief.one_thing_to_know: Nearly 80 MPs either voted no or abstained in protest — almost as many as voted yes.

cases.why_fail: Attributes motive to absences ('refusing to support', 'abstained in protest') — absences can be pairing, illness, travel. And '80 MPs' arithmetic merges two different things to manufacture a near-majority narrative.

cases.origin: CONTROL — correct handling of procedural vote, must PASS
cases.evidence:
News stories given to the writer:
- "Labor's tax reform package heads to committee scrutiny" [economy] — 12 sources

Vote records given to the writer:
- Treasury Laws Amendment (Tax Reform No. 1) Bill 2026 and another - Reference to Committee - Adjourn debate: passed (91–48)

what_happened: Labor's tax reform bills were sent to committee for detailed scrutiny, with the procedural vote passing 91–48.
brief.what_it_means: Tax changes are still moving through the process — nothing is law yet.
brief.one_thing_to_know: That 91–48 vote wasn't on the tax changes themselves — it was a procedural step sending the bills to committee.

cases.why_pass: Correctly describes the committee referral as procedural, exact tally, attribution grounded in the story headline. An over-strict grader failing this is a bug.

cases.origin: CONTROL — casual paraphrase of real evidence, must PASS
cases.evidence:
News stories given to the writer:
- "Vaping reform bill debated" [health] — 11 sources
- "Small business support package" [economy] — 6 sources

Vote records given to the writer:
- Therapeutic Goods and Other Legislation Amendment (Vaping Reforms) Bill - Second Reading - Criticises Government: defeated (56–84)
- Treasury Laws Amendment (Support for Small Business and Charities) Bill - Consideration of Senate Message: passed (75–18)

what_happened: An attempt to criticise the government's vaping reforms was voted down 56–84.
A small business support measure cleared another hurdle, passing 75–18.
brief.what_it_means: Vaping rules keep moving the government's way, and small business support is close to done.
brief.one_thing_to_know: These two votes went very differently — the small business measure passed 75 votes to 18, while the vaping criticism motion was defeated 56 votes to 84.

cases.why_pass: Casual but every claim traces to evidence; the 'one thing' restates only the two given tallies, no outside facts. Simplification and tone are the brief's job.
